FERS RETIREMENT

Maximize Your Federal Pension

Your FERS pension is the foundation of your federal retirement. We help you understand your options, optimize your retirement date, and maximize your lifetime benefits.

CHAPTER I: FERS FUNDAMENTALS

Your Foundation for Retirement

The Federal Employees Retirement System forms the cornerstone of your federal retirement. Understanding when you can retire, how your annuity is calculated, and how to optimize your benefits requires specialized expertise.

Schedule FERS Analysis

"Your FERS annuity is the foundation. Everything else builds on top of it."

— Mike Stevens, IAR

On This Page

  • Retirement Eligibility
  • Annuity Calculation
  • FERS Supplement
  • Survivor Benefits
  • Military Buyback
  • Optimization Strategies
CHAPTER II: ELIGIBILITY

When Can You Retire?

FERS offers multiple retirement pathways, each with different requirements and benefit calculations. Understanding your options is crucial for optimizing your retirement date and maximizing lifetime benefits.

Immediate Retirement Options

Option Age Requirement Service Requirement Pension Reduction
Age 62 + 5 years 62 5 years None
Age 60 + 20 years 60 20 years None
MRA + 30 years Your MRA 30 years None + FERS Supplement
MRA + 10 years Your MRA 10-29 years 5% per year under 62

Your Minimum Retirement Age (MRA) depends on your birth year, ranging from 56 to 57. For federal employees born between 1953-1964, the MRA is 56. For those born in 1970 or later, it's 57.

30 Years for Full Benefits
$1,800 Avg Monthly FERS Supplement
62 Age When Supplement Ends
CHAPTER III: CALCULATION

The Formula Behind Your Pension

Your FERS annuity calculation follows a specific formula, but the details matter significantly. Small changes in timing, service credit, or salary can impact your lifetime benefits by thousands.

FERS Annuity Formula

Years of Service × High-3 Salary × Multiplier

Standard Multiplier

1.0%

Most employees

Enhanced Multiplier

1.1%

Age 62+ with 20+ years

Special Category

1.7%

LEO/ATC/Fire (first 20 yrs)

Your high-3 average salary is calculated using your highest average basic pay during any 3 consecutive years of federal service. This includes locality pay but excludes overtime, bonuses, and other premium payments.

From Mike's book: "Tom Merrick learned the hard way that his final promotion pushed him into a higher tax bracket without significantly increasing his high-3 — the timing cost him thousands in net retirement income."

CHAPTER IV: THE BRIDGE

FERS Supplement Strategy

The FERS supplement bridges the income gap from retirement until you're eligible for Social Security at age 62. This benefit can provide $1,200-$2,000 per month, but it comes with specific eligibility requirements and income limitations.

FERS Supplement Eligibility

  • MRA + 30 years: Full supplement with no reduction
  • Age 60 + 20 years: Full supplement with no reduction
  • Annual Earnings Test: $1 reduction for every $2 over $23,400 (2025)

The supplement is subject to the Social Security earnings test — if you work after retirement and earn more than the annual limit, your supplement is reduced. This makes post-retirement employment planning crucial for supplement recipients.

Important: The FERS supplement automatically ends when you turn 62, regardless of when you actually claim Social Security. This creates a potential income gap that must be planned for in advance.

CHAPTER V: PROTECTION

Survivor Benefits Strategy

Your FERS survivor benefit election is irrevocable and significantly impacts both your retirement income and your spouse's financial security. This decision requires careful analysis of your specific situation.

Survivor Benefit Option Cost to Retiree Survivor Receives Best For
Full Survivor Benefit 10% reduction 50% of annuity Spouse depends on pension income
Partial Survivor Benefit 5% reduction 25% of annuity Some spouse protection needed
No Survivor Benefit No reduction Nothing Spouse has independent retirement

Many federal employees automatically choose the full survivor benefit without analyzing alternatives. Life insurance, spousal benefits, and other assets may provide better value than reducing your pension by 10% for life.

CHAPTER VI: SERVICE CREDIT

Military Buyback Analysis

Buying back military service credit can significantly increase your FERS annuity by adding years to your creditable service calculation. The decision requires a detailed cost-benefit analysis based on your specific situation.

3% Base Military Pay Cost
$8,000 Example Buyback Cost
$140,000 Lifetime Pension Increase

From Mike's book: The military buyback example shows a federal employee who paid $8,000 to buy back 4 years of military service, which increased their annual pension by $2,400 and provided over $140,000 in additional lifetime benefits.

Key Considerations

  • Payback Period: How long to recover the deposit cost
  • Life Expectancy: Total lifetime value of increased benefits
  • Interest Timing: Deposit within 3 years avoids interest charges
  • Survivor Impact: Increased benefits for spouse if elected
CHAPTER VII: OPTIMIZATION

Maximizing Your FERS Benefits

Optimizing your FERS benefits requires coordinating multiple moving pieces: retirement timing, high-3 salary management, sick leave conversion, and benefit elections. Small optimizations can result in significant lifetime value.

Optimization Strategies

Timing Strategies

  • Retirement date optimization
  • High-3 salary timing
  • Sick leave maximization
  • Promotional timing analysis

Benefit Coordination

  • TSP withdrawal sequencing
  • Social Security timing
  • Tax minimization strategies
  • Healthcare continuation planning

Your FERS pension includes Cost of Living Adjustments (COLAs) that protect against inflation. The COLA formula provides full adjustments when inflation is 2% or less, caps adjustments at 2% when inflation is 2.1-3%, and provides inflation minus 1% when inflation exceeds 3%.

CHAPTER VIII: COMMON QUESTIONS

FERS Planning FAQ

Everything you need to know about FERS retirement and your federal pension planning.

Schedule FERS Review

Your FERS annuity is calculated using the formula: years of creditable service × high-3 average salary × multiplier. The standard multiplier is 1% for most employees. If you retire at age 62 or older with at least 20 years of service, the multiplier increases to 1.1%. Special category employees (law enforcement, firefighters, air traffic controllers) receive 1.7% for the first 20 years and 1% thereafter. Your high-3 is your highest average basic pay during any 3 consecutive years of service.

The FERS supplement is a special benefit that approximates the Social Security benefit you earned while employed under FERS. It's designed to bridge the income gap from retirement until you're eligible for Social Security at age 62. You only receive the supplement if you retire at your Minimum Retirement Age (MRA) with 30 years of service, or at age 60 with 20 years. The supplement ends when you turn 62, regardless of when you claim Social Security.

FERS offers several retirement options depending on your age and service. Immediate retirement is available at age 62 with 5 years of service, age 60 with 20 years, or at your MRA with 30 years. You can also retire at your MRA with 10-29 years, but you'll face a 5% per year reduction for each year under age 62. Your MRA depends on your birth year — it's age 56 for those born 1953-1964, and gradually increases to age 57 for those born 1970 or later.

Buying back military service credit can increase your FERS annuity by adding years to your creditable service calculation. The cost is typically 3% of your military base pay, plus interest if you make the deposit after your first three years of federal civilian service. Whether it makes financial sense depends on your specific situation — how much service time you'd add, how close you are to retirement, and your life expectancy. We can calculate the exact payback period and lifetime value for your situation.

If you leave federal service before meeting immediate retirement eligibility, you have options depending on your years of service. With at least 5 years of FERS coverage, you can leave your contributions in the system and receive a deferred retirement benefit starting at age 62. With 10 or more years, you can begin receiving benefits at your MRA, though with a reduction if you're under 62. Alternatively, you can request a refund of your FERS contributions, but this means forfeiting your future annuity and the government's contributions.
